annotations.text: The distinctive constellation of findings in this case suggests the diagnosis of infection with  *Brucella melitensis*

A traveler returning from the Middle East who presents with a nonspecific, protracted febrile illness may have any one of a variety of bacterial, parasitic, rickettsial, fungal, viral, or noninfectious diseases.
If this patient had brucellosis, why did his disease relapse? The most likely explanation is inappropriate monotherapy with a quinolone (ciprofloxacin) and a cephalosporin (cefuroxime).

Two prospective studies in Spain identified several independent risk factors for a relapse of brucellosis: suboptimal antibiotic therapy, male sex, and various clinical markers indicative of severe infection (positive blood cultures during the initial infection, illness for less than 10 days before the start of treatment, thrombocytopenia, and a high initial temperature).
This patient had almost all these risk factors.
The clinical findings are compatible with the presence of  *B. melitensis* infection for several reasons
First, the protean, frequently elusive, nonspecific manifestations of this disease, including a nonspecific febrile syndrome without localizing signs and symptoms, have long been recognized.
The possibility of early spondylitis due to brucella merits consideration in this patient because it is very common in brucellosis.
the absence of sacroiliac- or sternoclavicular-joint involvement makes the diagnosis of brucella arthritis unlikely.
Extrapulmonary tuberculosis can simulate brucellosis, especially when the spine is involved.

A diagnosis of brucellosis does not exclude the possibility of associated tuberculosis, and a partial response to treatment for brucellosis with drugs that have activity against mycobacteria, such as rifampin, streptomycin, and quinolones, may make it difficult to establish a diagnosis of concomitant tuberculosis.
Plain radiographs and CT or magnetic resonance imaging studies occasionally help differentiate between osseous tuberculosis and brucellosis.
In summary, the most likely diagnosis in this case is subacute, partially treated brucellosis caused by  *B. melitensis*
Confirmatory studies could include a serologic test for brucella species and identification of the gram-negative bacillus in the patient's blood as  *B. melitensis*
